abstract: Across a 1000 km precipitation transect through the Brazilian Cerrado biome, topsoil soil organic carbon (SOC) in native savanna plots increased linearly with mean annual precipitation (MAP) between 900 and 1700 mm, after controlling for temperature and clay content. Higher MAP drives greater above-ground biomass input and slows decomposition through seasonal moisture regulation. Elevation exerted no direct effect on SOC once precipitation and temperature were adjusted for, confirming that the MAP-SOC pathway is largely mediated by primary productivity rather than by topographic position.

In deeply weathered Oxisols of the central Brazilian plateau, clay content at the Bt horizon is controlled primarily by parent material: gneiss-derived soils accumulated 45-60% clay by mass, whereas sandstone-derived profiles stabilised at 20-30%. Long-term weathering under warm and wet climate accelerates kaolinitisation; time since parent-material exposure and temperature jointly govern the rate of primary-mineral dissolution. The resulting argillic horizon in turn controls effective Cation Exchange Capacity.

Topographic position modulates soil water availability in undulating Cerrado landscapes. Slope angle determines local drainage; the Topographic Wetness Index (TWI) derived from digital elevation models captures this. TWI is positively associated with surface-layer soil moisture and negatively associated with bulk density. In turn, higher TWI promotes organic-matter retention by slowing aerobic decomposition, so TWI is a proxy covariate for SOC in this landscape.

Conversion of native Cerrado vegetation to continuous pasture reduced topsoil SOC stocks by 21-38% within 15 years, even without concomitant erosion. The dominant mechanism is reduced below-ground biomass input under shallow-rooted pasture grasses relative to the deep-rooted native savanna. Conversion to no-till soybean-maize rotation produced an intermediate SOC loss of 8-18%. Land use therefore affects SOC through altered root inputs.

On cultivated slopes exceeding 8% in the Cerrado, annual sheet-erosion rates exceeded 12 t ha-1 yr-1 and were positively correlated with slope angle. Erosion preferentially mobilises the SOC-enriched topsoil, contributing directly to SOC depletion at eroded sites. Vegetative buffer strips at the slope break reduced erosion by 60% and mitigated SOC loss. Hence slope drives erosion, and erosion drives SOC loss.

Annual dry-season fires, a defining feature of the Cerrado ecosystem, removed between 30% and 70% of surface necromass and translated into a decadal-scale reduction of topsoil SOC at high-frequency burn sites. Fire frequency was the single largest driver of SOC differences across protected and unmanaged savanna plots, after controlling for MAP and clay content. Increased fire frequency decreases topsoil SOC.

Soil clay content was the primary determinant of plant-available water capacity across 240 Cerrado Oxisol and Latosol profiles. Higher clay content increased water retention in the 0-40 cm layer, and this in turn raised maize grain yield under rainfed management, particularly during late-season dry spells. The clay - water retention - yield chain is therefore well established.

Landsat-derived dry-season NDVI showed strong positive correlation with both TWI and MAP across Cerrado protected reserves. Precipitation and topographic position jointly drive vegetation greenness; NDVI is therefore a useful synthesis covariate, but not a direct cause of SOC. Any regression of SOC on NDVI must adjust for MAP, slope and TWI as common ancestors.

Across Cerrado Oxisols, soil pH ranged from 4.2 to 6.5 and was strongly negatively correlated with exchangeable aluminium. Low pH reduces phosphorus availability and inhibits root development. Liming raises pH and increases both exchangeable Ca and available P, with downstream benefits for productivity. Parent-material weathering and leaching jointly control baseline pH.

Laboratory incubations of Cerrado topsoils showed that heterotrophic CO2 efflux followed a Q10 near 2.1 for temperatures between 18 and 32 degrees Celsius. Higher soil temperature accelerates microbial decomposition of labile SOC, shortening carbon residence time. On hillslopes, south-facing microclimates therefore accumulate more SOC than north-facing slopes at similar altitude.
